plantings.variety: Super Star + Yellow Sweet Spanish (~18 each)

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Runner management
tasks.description: Everbearing strawberries produce runners that root nearby. In a 2x4 raised bed, remove most runners to keep plant energy directed toward fruit production. Keep 1-2 runners per plant maximum if you want to propagate new plants.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Inspect for slugs
tasks.description: Slugs eat into ripening berries overnight — look for ragged holes in fruit and slime trails on soil surface. Pick berries slightly underripe if slug pressure is high. Diatomaceous earth scattered around bed perimeter helps as a barrier.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Shelling peas: pods should feel full and firm with peas visible through the pod wall. Check every 2 days once pods start forming.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Harvest when pod is plump but before interior peas bulge too large — sweetness peaks here and drops off fast. Check every 2 days once pods are sizing up.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Shelling peas: pods should feel full and firm with peas visible through the pod wall. Check every 2 days once pods start forming.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Pre-fruit feed — bone meal
tasks.description: Side-dress 1 tbsp bone meal per plant once first flowers appear. Supports fruit set and meets phosphorus demand during flowering.

tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
tasks.description: Check growing tips and undersides of upper leaves. For aphids: blast with hose or diluted dish soap. If caterpillar pests present: apply Bt per label. Avoid strong insecticides near flowering plants.

tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
tasks.description: Check growing tips and undersides of upper leaves for aphid colonies or caterpillars. For aphids: blast off with hose or spray diluted dish soap solution. If caterpillar pests present: apply Bt per label. Avoid strong insecticides — pollinators are working these flowers.

tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
tasks.description: Check growing tips and undersides of upper leaves for aphid colonies or caterpillars. For aphids: blast off with hose or spray diluted dish soap solution. If caterpillar pests present: apply Bt per label. Avoid strong insecticides — pollinators are working these flowers.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Mid-season feed — blood meal
tasks.description: Work 1 tbsp blood meal per plant into top inch of soil. Apply once heavy fruiting begins to compensate for nitrogen demand. Skip if foliage is already very dark green — excess nitrogen pushes leaf growth at expense of fruit.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Mid-season feed — fish emulsion
tasks.description: Half-strength fish emulsion (1 tbsp per gallon) once in early June to support continuous fruiting through summer. Do not overfeed — excess nitrogen pushes foliage at the expense of berry production.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Roma and paste types: firm and fully colored. Slicers: slight give when squeezed. Do not wait for perfect color in heat — fully vine-ripened tomatoes go soft fast in summer temperatures.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Stop watering
tasks.description: Stop all irrigation 2 weeks before expected harvest. Dry conditions cure the outer wrapper and improve storage life significantly. Italian Red (softneck, W4) may be ready a week or two before Elephant & Music (hardneck, W3).

tasks.title: Stop watering
tasks.description: Stop all irrigation 2 weeks before expected harvest. Dry conditions cure the outer wrapper and improve storage life significantly. Italian Red (softneck, W4) may be ready a week or two before Elephant & Music (hardneck, W3).

tasks.title: Harvest check
tasks.description: Harvest when lower 3-4 leaves have browned but upper 5-6 are still green. Do not wait until all leaves are brown — the wrapper will deteriorate. Dig carefully with a fork, do not pull by the stem.

tasks.title: Harvest check
tasks.description: Harvest when lower 3-4 leaves have browned but upper 5-6 are still green. Do not wait until all leaves are brown — the wrapper will deteriorate. Dig carefully with a fork, do not pull by the stem.

tasks.title: Fall renovation
tasks.description: After last harvest in fall, cut all foliage back to 1 inch above crown. Remove old mulch and debris. Add 1 inch of fresh compost to the bed. Apply fall Berry-Tone feeding at label rate. This resets the plant for next year.

tasks.title: Early spring feed — Berry-Tone
tasks.description: Apply True Organic Berry-Tone at label rate (approximately 1/2 cup per 5 sq ft of bed) as soon as new growth appears in early spring. Work lightly into top inch of soil and water in well.

payload.headline: Radish harvest day; prepare for multi-day rain event.
insights: The '18-Day French' radishes have reached their expected harvest date, indicating successful early season growth.
Pea plants across three varieties are showing healthy tendril development, suggesting vigorous establishment.
Newly planted strawberries and tomatoes, though still in 'sown' status, are facing their first significant weather challenge with high winds.
Garlic plantings continue to thrive, benefiting from consistent moisture and recent fish emulsion application.
The garden has received consistent early spring care, including recent worm castings and fish emulsion applications to various beds.
payload.narrative:
The garden is actively growing this spring! Your garlic plantings are robustly establishing, while pea varieties are thriving, showing their characteristic 'tentacles'. Newly sown strawberries and tomatoes are also settling into their beds, marking the start of a busy growing season. Critically, your '18-Day French' radishes are now ready for their first harvest today, and your 'Orange Hat' tomato needs a sucker check.

Looking ahead, you'll need to inspect all your pea plants for aphids around April 17th. This week's weather will significantly impact outdoor activities. Strong winds are expected today, which could stress your younger plants, so temporary protection might be beneficial.

Expect a good soaking from the sky over the next few days. Showers and thunderstorms are forecast from today through Tuesday. This means you can hold off on any manual watering, but it also necessitates careful planning for other tasks. Temperatures will be mild today before cooling down for Monday and Tuesday, with overnight lows hovering in the upper 30s. Tasks like pest inspection on peas should likely be scheduled after the rain subsides.

task_timing.observation: Prioritize harvesting '18-Day French' radishes today, ideally before heavy rain starts later this afternoon.
Complete the scheduled 'Orange Hat' tomato sucker check today, being mindful of the strong winds.
Delay all manual watering until after Tuesday due to the significant precipitation expected.
Consider rescheduling pea aphid inspections (due Apr 17) to after the rainy period, around Wednesday or Thursday, for better efficacy.

weather_notes: Significant rain and thunderstorms are expected today through Tuesday, providing natural irrigation.
Strong winds with gusts up to 38 mph are forecast for today, potentially stressing young plants.
Temperatures will drop after today, with highs in the upper 50s on Monday and Tuesday, and overnight lows near 38-39°F.
Wednesday offers a clearer, warmer window for garden activities after the rain.

suggested_tasks.title: Harvest 18-Day French Radish
suggested_tasks.description: Your '18-Day French' radishes are ready for harvest! Pull them today for peak flavor and tenderness before they become woody. Check the size and taste test a few.

suggested_tasks.title: Protect Strawberries from wind
suggested_tasks.description: High winds today could stress your newly sown 'Everbearing' strawberry plants. Consider temporary windbreaks or row covers.

suggested_tasks.title: Protect Tomato from wind
suggested_tasks.description: Strong winds today could stress your recently planted 'Orange Hat' tomato. Consider temporary windbreaks or support.

payload.headline: Warm weather boosts growth; radishes nearing harvest, peas thriving.
insights: Radishes are on track for harvest very soon, benefiting from recent warmth and feeding.
All pea varieties ('Lillian's Caseload', 'Lincoln Garden', 'Sugar Bon') are showing strong initial growth and benefiting from the recent fish emulsion application.
Recently planted 'Everbearing' strawberries and 'Orange Hat' tomato will be establishing roots and foliage during this warm period.
Garlic crops are well-established and growing steadily, having received a fish emulsion application recently.
The garden is progressing nicely into the spring season with most plantings active and showing good signs of development.
payload.narrative:
The garden is experiencing a rapid growth spurt with unseasonably warm temperatures. Your recently planted peas are showing good progress with their characteristic 'tentacles,' and the '18-Day French' radish crop is quickly nearing its first harvest. Established garlic plants are also benefiting from the warmer conditions, setting the stage for a productive spring.

This week, your top priority is the '18-Day French' radish, which is expected to be ready for harvest around April 12th. Keep a close eye on your young tomato plant for suckers, with the first check due Sunday. Ensure all newly planted crops, especially the strawberries and tomato, receive adequate moisture during this warm stretch before the weekend rain.

The forecast promises two very warm days in the mid-70s, which will accelerate plant growth, especially for your spring crops. This warmth will be followed by a significant chance of rain and thunderstorms on Saturday, bringing cooler temperatures. This natural watering will be beneficial, but be mindful of any potential strong winds during the storms.

task_timing.observation: Given the warm temperatures today and tomorrow, it's advisable to complete any necessary watering of new plantings (Strawberries, Tomato) before Saturday's forecasted heavy rain to ensure they are well-hydrated.
Begin daily checks for your '18-Day French' radish, as the warm weather will likely accelerate their readiness for harvest by the expected date of April 12.
The first 'Check for suckers' task for the 'Orange Hat' Tomato is due on April 12. This can be performed any time between now and then, potentially on Friday before the rain.

weather_notes: Unseasonably warm temperatures (mid-70s) expected for Thursday and Friday.
A 70% chance of rain and thunderstorms is forecasted for Saturday.
Temperatures will drop significantly after Saturday's rain to the low 40s at night.
Light to moderate winds (up to 15 mph) expected Friday and Saturday.

suggested_tasks.title: Deep water new strawberries
suggested_tasks.description: Ensure your newly planted 'Everbearing' strawberries are thoroughly watered to help them establish roots and cope with the upcoming warm spell.

suggested_tasks.title: Deep water young tomato
suggested_tasks.description: The young 'Orange Hat' tomato plant will benefit from deep watering to prevent stress from high temperatures and encourage strong root development.

suggested_tasks.title: Check radish for harvest
suggested_tasks.description: Your '18-Day French' radishes are nearing their expected harvest date. Begin checking daily for root swelling and harvest when they reach desired size to prevent woodiness.
